title: NOC 85102 – Aquaculture and marine harvest labourers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 85102 encompasses the essential frontline workforce of Canada’s "Blue Economy." As a TEER 5 occupation, these labourers provide the manual support required to cultivate, grow, and harvest fish, shellfish, and marine plants in both land-based hatcheries and open-water sea farms. In 2026, as Canada positions itself as a global leader in sustainable seafood production, these workers are in high demand to support the expansion of Atlantic salmon, mussel, and oyster farms. For immigration, this role is a primary entry point through the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the New 2026 Sector-Specific Stream for Agriculture and Fish Processing.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level5Major Group85 – Harvesting, landscaping and natural resources labourersSub-Major Group851 – Harvesting, landscaping and natural resources labourersMinor Group8510 – Agriculture, horticulture and harvesting labourersNOC Code85102

duties: Cultivation Support: Assist in the daily care of fish and shellfish by distributing feed and monitoring water oxygen levels.
Harvesting: Manually harvest shellfish (e.g., oysters, clams, mussels) from beaches, beds, or collector strings.
Maintenance: Clean and maintain sea cages, nets, and hatchery tanks; assist in the repair of specialized aquaculture gear.
Processing Support: Sort and grade harvested seafood by size and quality; pack products into crates or bags for transport to processing plants.
Hatchery Assistance: Assist in the spawning and transfer of fry and fingerlings between tanks and sea cages.
Safety: Adhere to strict biosecurity and maritime safety protocols to protect both the stock and the workers.
employment_requirements: No specific education or training requirements are mandatory.
Short work demonstration and on-the-job training are provided.
Marine Emergency Duties (MED) certificate may be required for those working on vessels or offshore platforms.
Physical stamina and the ability to work in outdoor, wet, and sometimes remote environments are essential.
Basic communication skills in English or French for safety compliance.
salary_prospects: Note: Many shellfish harvesters work on a "piece-rate" basis, where earnings are determined by the volume of the harvest, allowing for higher potential income during peak seasons.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. According to the Department of Fisheries and Oceans (DFO), aquaculture is the fastest-growing food production sector in Canada. As land-based Recirculating Aquaculture Systems (RAS) continue to expand in provinces like Ontario and Nova Scotia, the demand for year-round indoor hatchery labourers is expected to increase significantly through 2027.
labour_market_demand: The demand for aquaculture labourers is Good to Very Good, particularly in coastal provinces. In 2026, British Columbia, New Brunswick, and Newfoundland and Labrador are reporting steady vacancies as the sector transitions toward more intensive, year-round production cycles. With Canada’s commitment to "sustainable aquaculture growth," many firms are actively recruiting international workers to support the daily operations of high-tech hatcheries and offshore cages.
High Accessibility: Minimal formal education or experience required, making it an ideal "starter" role in the Canadian maritime sector.
Technological Exposure: Work with modern automated feeding systems, underwater monitoring cameras, and advanced water filtration technology.
Career Progression: Gain the foundational experience needed to advance to Aquaculture Operator (NOC 84121) or Aquaculture Manager (NOC 80022).
Direct Path to PR: Recognized as an "Essential Service" in Atlantic Canada, offering unique pathways to Permanent Residency through regional immigration programs.
pathways: New 2026 Sector-Specific Stream: IRCC has introduced a dedicated PR stream for agriculture and fish processing, specifically including aquaculture labourers.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-priority route for workers with job offers from designated aquaculture employers in the Atlantic region.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Accessible for those working in the province's fishing and aquaculture sectors for at least 9 months.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Targeted at workers moving to maritime-dependent rural communities.
